message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 30
Recent Green phase: wrapped final return in Math.floor for payout rounding

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll execute the Refactor phase systematically.

content.content:
architecture-notes.md
eslint.config.mjs
example-mapping
metrics.json
node_modules
package.json
pnpm-lock.yaml
prompt.md
run.log
src
tdd-journal.md
tsconfig.json
vitest.config.ts

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted module-level constant `PROCESSING_FEE = 5` in `src/claim-office.ts`; `quote()` now returns `PROCESSING_FEE`
5	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `5` is the domain "processing fee" from the spec; naming it removes a magic number
6	- Naming established: `PROCESSING_FEE` (module-private constant); `quote(input)` public function kept as-is — name still fits
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- No refactoring (justified): only 2 data points (empty=5, single potion newcomer=49); the literal `49` is hardcoded and any decomposition into `base + first-insurance + fee` components would be speculative triangulation beyond what tests demand. Naming `49` (e.g. `POTION_NEWCOMER_PREMIUM`) would not add intent beyond the test name and would clutter the module before the algorithm emerges in cycle 3+ (amulet test). `quote` name still fits.
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: replaced `if (type === "amulet") return 71; return 49;` chain with a `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE: Record<string, number>` lookup table (`potion: 49, amulet: 71`); `quote` now does a single map lookup on `items[0].type`
13	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — premium varies per item type, so a per-type lookup is the natural shape and removes a conditional chain that would have grown with staff/sword. Also nets Rule 4 (one fewer conditional, mass 23→22). Kept totals (49, 71) un-decomposed: tests do not yet pin down the `base + base/10 + fee` formula; decomposition deferred to cycle 4 (staff) or 5 (sword) when a 3rd/4th data point makes the pattern undeniable
14	- Naming established: `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (module-private lookup); `quote` name still fits
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- Change: decomposed opaque per-type totals into the domain formula. `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (49/71/93) → `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (40/60/80) plus new constant `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.1`. `quote` now computes `basePrice + basePrice * 0.1 + PROCESSING_FEE` for non-empty item lists.
18	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — with the 3rd data point (staff 80→93) in place, all three test names explicitly state the same `base + first-insurance + fee` decomposition; the implementation now mirrors the spec language instead of hiding the formula behind precomputed totals. Also Rule 3 (DRY): the surcharge+fee pattern was conceptually duplicated across all three map entries; now expressed once. Mass increased ~25→~33 — accepted because Rule 2 > APP. Verified all 3 base prices (40, 60, 80) and the upcoming sword case (100) yield integer results under `base*0.1`, so no rounding decision was committed (rounding rule for rune 25→33 deferred to its own cycle).
19	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (replaces `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE`), `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` (new domain constant); `quote` name still fits
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): sword test added one row (`sword: 100`) to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` — the cycle-4 decomposition absorbed it without any structural change. Considered inlining `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * (1 + RATE)` for Rule 4 (fewest elements), rejected because test names enumerate three distinct components ("100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee") and Rule 2 (reveals intent) > APP. Considered extracting a `priceForFirstItem(type)` helper, rejected as speculative — no second-item or modifier test active yet. `quote` name still fits.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: extracted `const premium = basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE` so the final return reads `return Math.ceil(premium)` instead of `Math.ceil(basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE)`. Also added `rune: 25` to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (in Green); the structural fit was already there.
26	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the rune test introduced rounding-up as a domain rule (25 × 1.1 + 5 = 32.5 → 33). Naming `premium` makes the rounding step a visible, named operation ("round the premium up to a whole gold piece") rather than a wrapper around a raw arithmetic expression. Mass +1 binding (~35→36), accepted on the cycle-4 precedent that Rule 2 > APP when test vocabulary ("premium ... rounded up") explicitly names the value. Considered leaving the inline form for Rule 4 (fewest elements) — rejected because the rounding rule's target deserves a name now that rounding exists. Considered renaming `quote` itself (e.g. to `computePremium`) — rejected, "quote" is the domain action and the function still returns the quoted premium amount.
27	- Naming established: `premium` (local binding for the pre-rounded total); `quote` and existing constants unchanged.
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- Change: removed the empty-items early-return guard (`if (input.items.length === 0) return PROCESSING_FEE;`). With cycle-7 Green generalising `basePrice` to `items.reduce(..., 0)`, the empty case now falls out of the same formula: `0 + 0*0.1 + 5 = 5` (and `Math.ceil(5) = 5`). One uniform code path handles all item counts.
31	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 4 (fewest elements) — the special case became dead code the moment `basePrice` became an accumulator over `items`. Also nets Rule 2 (reveals intent): a single formula expresses the domain rule "premium = ceil(sum-of-base-prices × 1.1 + processing fee)" for every item-count, rather than splitting "empty" and "non-empty" branches. Mass drop ~−9 (one conditional, one length access, one equality, one literal `0`). Considered keeping the guard for "documentation value" — rejected: the empty test name ("empty item list → premium 5 G (processing fee only)") is now an emergent consequence of the general formula, which is stronger evidence of correctness than a hand-coded shortcut. Naming all still fits.
32	- Naming established: none new; removed elements only.
33	
34	## After cycle 8
35	- Change: extracted the literal `60` in the 3-runes guard into module-level constant `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`. Body now reads `isThreeAlikeRunes ? THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE : rawBasePrice` instead of `... ? 60 : rawBasePrice`. Implementation shape (narrow guard `isThreeAlikeRunes`) left intact.
36	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `60` is the domain "building block price for 3 runes" per Example Mapping; naming removes a magic number and the visual collision with `amulet: 60` (different concepts, same value). Mass +1 (one new constant binding at module scope), accepted on Rule 2 > APP precedent. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `hasRuneBuildingBlock` and generalising to a per-block discount (would scale to cycles 9-12: 4 runes, 7 runes, mixed rune/moonstone). Rejected as speculative — next test ("4 runes → no block") will force the generalisation with concrete data; doing it now would be triangulation. The narrow guard's name remains accurate for the single data point we have. Considered also extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` into a named predicate function — rejected, it's used once and inlining preserves locality.
37	- Naming established: `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` (module-private constant); `isThreeAlikeRunes`, `quote`, and other existing names unchanged.
38	
39	## After cycle 9
40	- Change: extracted `const items = input.items` at the top of `quote` to remove three repeated `input.items` accesses across the reduce, the length check, and the every-call. Body now reads `items.reduce(...)`, `items.length === 3 && items.every(...)`.
41	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 3 (no duplication) — three identical property accesses become one named binding; the predicate line in particular reads more cleanly without the repeated `input.` prefix. Small APP win (~−3 mass: drop two `input.items` accesses, add one binding). The 4-runes test added no implementation change — the cycle-8 narrow guard `length === 3` already excluded it — confirming that test 9 is the negative-case confirmation, NOT the forcing test for generalisation. Considered (and rejected) generalising the rune-block logic to `floor(runeCount/3)` blocks now; that is speculative triangulation. The 7-runes cycle (cycle 10, currently `it.todo` "7 runes (1 block of 3 + 4 individuals) → 175 G base") is the genuine forcing test for that generalisation. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes`: name still accurate after a negative-case confirmation. Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function or inlining `rawBasePrice`: both rejected on cycle-8 precedent (locality / verbose-expression-deserves-a-name respectively).
42	- Naming established: `items` (local binding aliasing `input.items`); no other name changes.
43	
44	## After cycle 10
45	- No refactoring (justified): the 7-runes test (expected base 175 = 7 × 25, premium 198) passed without any implementation change. The cycle-8 narrow guard `items.length === 3` excludes the 7-rune case from the block-discount path, so `rawBasePrice` (175) flows straight through `basePrice` and the existing formula yields ceil(175 + 17.5 + 5) = ceil(197.5) = 198. Important observation: the test's prose ("1 block of 3 + 4 individuals → 175 G base") describes the *narrative* but the *numeric expectation* (175 = 7 × 25, no discount) confirms that the block discount only applies when exactly 3 runes are insured — making this the SECOND confirming test for the narrow guard, not a forcing test. The genuine forcing test for `floor(runeCount/3)` generalisation is cycle 12 ("3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks → 120 G base"), where two distinct per-type blocks must each be priced at 60. Generalising now (computing `floor(count/3)` blocks across all runes only) would still need restructuring once moonstones arrive, so it's premature. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `qualifiesForThreeRuneBlock` (consequence-oriented over structure-oriented, marginal Rule 2 gain) — rejected because cycle 12 will force the predicate to change shape entirely (per-type rune+moonstone block counting), so any rename now is churn. Considered inlining `rawBasePrice` into the ternary or collapsing `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * 1.1` — both rejected on cycle-4/cycle-5 precedent that test-name vocabulary ("base + first-insurance + fee") earns each value its own named binding (Rule 2 > APP). Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function — rejected on cycle-8 precedent (used once, inlining preserves locality). All existing names still fit.
46	
47	## After cycle 11
48	- Change: reordered module-level constants by domain role. Now grouped as `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (with the new `moonstone: 25` entry added in Green) + `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` first (item-pricing data), followed by `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` + `PROCESSING_FEE` (premium-formula constants). Body of `quote` unchanged.
49	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the new ordering mirrors the function's computational flow (lookup base prices → maybe apply block override → apply surcharge → add fee), making the data shape easier to scan as cycle 12 will introduce per-type block-pricing logic. Mass change: 0 (pure reorder). The "2 runes + 1 moonstone → no block" test is the THIRD confirming test for the narrow `isThreeAlikeRunes` guard (after cycles 9 and 10): the `every((item) => item.type === "rune")` predicate correctly excludes the mixed-type case, so no structural change was needed. Cycle 12 ("3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks → 120 G base") remains the genuine forcing test that will restructure the predicate into per-type block counting. Considered (and rejected) generalising the predicate now — would be triangulation on three confirming negatives. Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a function or renaming it — rejected on cycle-8/10 precedent (used once, cycle-12 will reshape it). Considered deduplicating `rune: 25, moonstone: 25` via a shared constant — rejected as false coupling (the two types share a price by spec coincidence, not by definition). All existing names still fit.
50	- Naming established: none new; constant order is the only change.
51	
52	## After cycle 12
53	- Change: clarified the rune-component block-discount vocabulary. (1) Renamed `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` → `RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_PRICE` — the cycle-12 Green generalised the rule to apply to both runes AND moonstones, so the "THREE_RUNES" name had become a lie. (2) Extracted `RUNE_COMPONENT_TYPES = new Set(["rune", "moonstone"])` to name the spec category "rune-class building components". (3) Extracted `RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE = 3` to name the domain literal (exactly 3 of a kind forms a block). (4) Extracted local `const formsRuneBlock = RUNE_COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && count === RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE` to name the per-type-group predicate that the cycle-12 Green collapsed into the inline `if`.
54	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — after cycle 12, the inline guard `(type === "rune" || type === "moonstone") && count === 3` mixed three domain concepts (the rune-component type category, the block size, the block-formation predicate) as anonymous literals. Each now has a name that mirrors the spec language ("rune components", "block of three"). The renamed constant also resolves the stale "THREE_RUNES" name that no longer matched the moonstone case. Considered also generalising to `floor(count/blockSize)` blocks per type (would support a future "6 runes = 2 blocks at 120 G" test) — rejected as speculative triangulation; the current tests only pin down "exactly 3 of a kind". Considered renaming `RUNE_COMPONENT_TYPES` to something more general like `BLOCK_ELIGIBLE_TYPES` — rejected, "rune component" is the spec's noun phrase for runes+moonstones (per Example Mapping). Considered extracting a `priceForTypeGroup(type, count)` helper function — rejected on cycle-8/10 precedent (used once, locality preserved by inline ternary-ish branch). Mass change: ~+5 (3 new constant bindings + 1 local binding + 1 invocation `.has()` swapped in for `||`+`===`+`===` — net mass roughly neutral; accepted on Rule 2 > APP precedent). All structural shape (countByType + per-type-group loop + branch) preserved.
55	- Naming established: `RUNE_COMPONENT_TYPES` (Set replacing inline type union), `RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE` (replaces literal `3`), `RUNE_COMPONENT_BLOCK_PRICE` (renames `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE`), `formsRuneBlock` (local per-type-group predicate); `quote`, `countByType`, `basePrice`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `premium`, `items`, `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `PROCESSING_FEE` unchanged.
56	
57	## After cycle 13
58	- No refactoring (justified): the "sword enchantment 4, not cursed → 115 G" test passed immediately with zero implementation change — `enchantment` and `cursed` fields are not yet read, and the boundary-below-threshold case `4 < 5` correctly exercises the existing path (`sword: 100` base → 115 G premium) unchanged. This is the FIRST confirming test for the upcoming high-enchantment-surcharge rule, NOT its forcing test. Cycle 14 ("enchantment exactly 5 → +30 G") is the genuine forcing test for the high-enchantment surcharge; cycle 15 ("cursed → +50 G") is the forcing test for the curse surcharge. Considered widening the `items` type from `{ type: string }[]` to `{ type: string; enchantment?: number; cursed?: boolean }[]` to honour what the test actually passes — rejected as premature; the type should grow when those fields are actually consumed (cycle 14), and adding optional fields now with no reader would be speculative. Considered pre-extracting a `surchargesFor(item)` helper returning 0 — rejected as pure YAGNI. Considered extracting a per-type-group `priceForGroup(type, count)` helper, collapsing `firstInsuranceSurcharge`/`premium`/`items` locals, or renaming `quote` — all rejected on prior-cycle precedent (cycles 4, 6, 8, 10: test vocabulary earns each value its own name; locality preserved when used once; "quote" is the domain action). All existing names still fit. Mass unchanged.
59	
60	## After cycle 14
61	- Change: three coordinated improvements after the high-enchantment-surcharge forcing test (sword ench 5 → 145 G) went green. (1) Renamed misleading local `itemPremium` (which held `basePrice + per-item enchantment surcharges` — never a "premium") into a separate `highEnchantmentSurcharges` accumulator initialised to 0; the per-item loop now adds only the surcharge contribution. (2) Made the premium assembly explicit and ordered to mirror R10: `const premium = basePrice + highEnchantmentSurcharges + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE`. (3) Extracted module-level `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` (grouped above the existing policy-formula constants), removing two magic numbers (`5`, `0.3`).
62	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the post-Green `itemPremium` name was a lie (it was not a premium, just a mid-pipeline subtotal that conflated base price with one specific item-level surcharge). The new shape names each surcharge family separately, and the explicit four-summand `premium` line reads as R10's stacking order verbatim ("item base + item-specific surcharges + first-insurance surcharge + processing fee"). The constants match R5's exact spec phrasing ("enchantment level ≥ 5", "30% surcharge"). Setup benefit for cycle 16 (cursed surcharge): it will drop in as a parallel `cursedSurcharges` accumulator in the same per-item loop, slotted into the same `premium` sum, with no restructuring. Considered merging the two `for (const item of items)` loops (countByType + high-ench) into one pass for Rule 3 (DRY) — rejected: the block-discount step needs `countByType` fully built before computing `basePrice`, and only then can per-item-base lookups be meaningful; the two loops solve different sub-problems despite looping over the same collection. Considered keeping `itemPremium` as a rolling subtotal and only adding a comment — rejected: misleading names are a smell that comments cannot fix. Considered NOT naming `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD`/`_RATE` yet ("one data point") — rejected: cycle 13 (boundary 4) plus cycle 14 (exactly 5) together pin down both the threshold and the rate as named domain concepts, and the next two todos (cycles 15-16) reuse these exact values in test names ("enchantment 5", "30 high-ench"). Mass change: ~+5 (two new constants, one extra `+` in the premium line, one new binding `highEnchantmentSurcharges`; offset by literals→named-constants which keep mass weight the same). Accepted on cycle-4/6/8/12 precedent that Rule 2 > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the values.
63	- Naming established: `highEnchantmentSurcharges` (local per-item-surcharge accumulator, replaces misleading `itemPremium`), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (module-private constant, replaces literal `5`), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE` (module-private constant, replaces literal `0.3`); `quote`, `basePrice`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `premium`, `items`, `countByType`, and all R3/R7/R9 constants unchanged.
64	
65	## After cycle 15
66	- Change: extracted module-level `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5` (grouped with the existing `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_*` constants above the policy-formula block), replacing the literal `0.5` in the per-item cursed surcharge accumulator. The cycle-15 Green added `cursedSurcharges` as a parallel accumulator to `highEnchantmentSurcharges`, but with the surcharge rate as a magic literal `0.5` — asymmetric with the named `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` extracted in cycle 14.
67	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `0.5` is the domain "curse surcharge rate" per spec E6 ("cursed → +50% of base price"). The test name reads "100 + 50 curse + 30 high-ench"; both surcharges appear side-by-side in the premium and now both rates are named side-by-side at module scope, so the two surcharge lines read identically (`base * CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE` vs. `base * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`). Also Rule 3 (no duplication): the structural duplication between the two surcharge rules was visually obscured by one being named and the other being a literal; symmetric naming makes the parallel explicit and sets up cycle-16+ adding loyalty/follow-up rates in the same constant block. Mass change: ~+2 (one new binding + one new constant at module scope, offset by literal → named-binding swap in the loop). Accepted on cycle-14 precedent (Rule 2 > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the value). Considered merging the two surcharge `if`s into a rules array iteration — rejected as speculative and would obscure per-policy clarity. Considered merging the two `for (const item of items)` loops (countByType + per-item-surcharges) — rejected on cycle-14 precedent: the two loops solve different sub-problems (group-counting for block discount vs. per-item-instance surcharges) and the basePrice must be assembled before per-item lookups are meaningful in the eventual loyalty-discount path. Considered renaming `cursedSurcharges` → `curseSurcharges` for consistency with `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE` — kept `cursedSurcharges` since "cursed" is the spec adjective on items and the plural reads as "surcharges from cursed items". `quote` name and all other names still fit.
68	- Naming established: `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE` (module-private constant, replaces literal `0.5`); `cursedSurcharges` accumulator (introduced in Green, kept), `quote`, and all prior constants/locals unchanged.
69	
70	## After cycle 16
71	- Change: extracted two module-level constants `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2` and `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2` (grouped in a new customer-policy block between the item-surcharge rates and the premium-formula constants), replacing the two magic literals `2` and `0.2` in the cycle-16 Green's inline ternary. Also split the ternary into two lines: `const qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount = input.customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS;` and `const loyaltyDiscount = qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount ? basePrice * LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE : 0;`. The `premium` assembly line (subtracting `loyaltyDiscount`) and the rest of the module unchanged.
72	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the cycle-16 Green left `2` (years threshold per spec C2) and `0.2` (20% discount rate per spec C2) as bare literals, asymmetric with the named `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5` / `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` (cycle 14) and `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5` (cycle 15). The test name uses "loyalty discount" and the spec uses "2 years" / "20%" verbatim; both values earn names. Splitting `qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount` out of the ternary names the eligibility check as a separate domain concept (cycle 17, the boundary test "1 year → no loyalty", will read `< LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS` naturally; cycle 20's follow-up discount will likely add `FOLLOWUP_DISCOUNT_RATE` in the same constant block and possibly mirror the predicate). Also Rule 3 (no duplication): the implicit parallel between the loyalty discount and the upcoming follow-up discount (cycle 20: `−15 follow-up`) is now visually anchored to a named customer-policy section. Mass change: ~+4 (two new module constants + one new local binding, offset by two literal → named-constant swaps and ternary line-split). Accepted on cycle-14/15 precedent (Rule 2 > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the value). Considered keeping the single-line ternary for Rule 4 (fewest elements) — rejected: cycle 17's boundary test and cycle 18's "3 yrs first quote" both reuse the eligibility predicate semantically; naming it now prevents an awkward inline `(input.customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS)` repeated in the follow-up code path. Considered renaming `quote` to `computePremium` now that we have a customer-policy step — rejected, "quote" remains the spec verb and the function still returns the quoted premium. Considered extracting a `policyAdjustmentsFor(customer, basePrice)` helper that returns the net loyalty/follow-up delta — rejected as speculative; follow-up discount only arrives in cycle 20, and the current inline form keeps locality. Considered also renaming `loyaltyDiscount` → `loyaltyDiscountAmount` to disambiguate from the rate — rejected: "discount" already reads as an amount in the `premium` subtraction line, and the cycle-15 `cursedSurcharges` precedent keeps amounts as plain nouns and rates as `*_RATE`.
73	- Naming established: `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS` (module-private constant, replaces literal `2`), `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE` (module-private constant, replaces literal `0.2`), `qualifiesForLoyaltyDiscount` (local predicate binding); `loyaltyDiscount` (introduced in Green, kept), `quote`, and all prior constants/locals unchanged.
74	
75	## After cycle 17
76	- Change: three coordinated improvements after the follow-up-discount forcing test (3-yr customer's 2nd quote: cursed sword ench 7 with `previousQuoteCount: 1` → 160 G) went green. (1) Extracted module-level `FOLLOWUP_THRESHOLD_PREVIOUS_QUOTES = 1` (grouped with the existing `LOYALTY_*` customer-policy constants), replacing the literal `1` in the eligibility check. (2) Extracted module-level `FOLLOWUP_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.15` (also in the customer-policy block), replacing the literal `0.15` in the discount calculation. (3) Split the inline ternary into a named predicate `const qualifiesForFollowUpDiscount = (input.previousQuoteCount ?? 0) >= FOLLOWUP_THRESHOLD_PREVIOUS_QUOTES;` followed by `const followUpDiscount = qualifiesForFollowUpDiscount ? basePrice * FOLLOWUP_DISCOUNT_RATE : 0;`, making the follow-up code structurally identical to the cycle-16 loyalty pair.
77	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the cycle-17 Green left `1` (the "had at least one prior quote" threshold per spec C3) and `0.15` (the 15% follow-up rate per spec C3) as bare literals, asymmetric with the named `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2` / `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2` cycle-16 pair. Test name reads "−15 follow-up"; spec calls it "15% follow-up discount on subsequent quotes in the same scenario". Both values now earn names that mirror the spec vocabulary. Also Rule 3 (no duplication): the loyalty and follow-up discount rules are structural twins (predicate → conditional `basePrice * RATE` subtracted from `premium`); naming the follow-up predicate makes that parallel visually explicit — the two pairs now read in matching shape three lines apart. Considered NOT naming `FOLLOWUP_THRESHOLD_PREVIOUS_QUOTES` ("threshold value of 1 is obvious") — rejected: the literal `1` is opaque about *what* count it gates (previous quotes? items? years?) without the named binding, and future CLI scenario-scoped tracking (todo 38) will read this same threshold. Considered inlining the predicate (single-line ternary) for Rule 4 (fewest elements) — rejected on cycle-16 precedent: the loyalty predicate was split for the same reason (boundary-test legibility + symmetry with sibling discount), and follow-up should match. Considered merging both discounts into a `policyDiscounts` array iterated to compute a total — rejected as speculative; only two discounts exist and the inline `premium` line still reads at a glance. Considered renaming `quote` → `computePremium` now that we have 4 distinct sub-rules (item-base, item-surcharges, customer-discounts, formula constants) — rejected, "quote" remains the spec verb and the function still returns the quoted premium. Considered also merging `followUpDiscount` and `loyaltyDiscount` into a single `customerDiscounts` accumulator — rejected: each discount has its own rate and predicate sourced from different policy clauses (loyalty from years-with-MHPCO, follow-up from previous-quote-count); they're parallel but not duplicative, and keeping them named-and-subtracted-individually in the `premium` line keeps each clause traceable. Mass change: ~+3 (two new module-level constants + one new local binding `qualifiesForFollowUpDiscount`; offset by two literal → named-constant swaps and one ternary line-split). Accepted on cycle-14/15/16 precedent (Rule 2 > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the value).
78	- Naming established: `FOLLOWUP_THRESHOLD_PREVIOUS_QUOTES` (module-private constant, replaces literal `1`), `FOLLOWUP_DISCOUNT_RATE` (module-private constant, replaces literal `0.15`), `qualifiesForFollowUpDiscount` (local predicate binding); `followUpDiscount` (introduced in Green, kept), `quote`, and all prior constants/locals unchanged.
79	
80	## After cycle 18
81	- No refactoring (justified): the first `claim()` test (`steel sword, enchantment 3, damage 500 G → payout 400 G`) was green-implemented as `function claim(_input: unknown): number { return 400; }` — the canonical one-data-point hardcoded return. Considered four refactorings, rejected each: (1) Extract `DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM = 100` and decompose to `damages[0].amount - DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM` — speculative triangulation with a single data point; per cycle-2 precedent on `quote`, the deductible rule's forcing tests are the next two todos ("damaged rune, damage 200 → payout 100" and "dragon attack damages sword 500 and amulet 300 → 600 G with per-item deductibles"), which together pin down both the deductible amount AND the per-item application. Decomposing now would commit to a specific shape (single-item subtract) that the next test will need to reshape into a per-damage-entry reduce. (2) Rename `claim` → `computePayout` — rejected on cycle-6 precedent for `quote`: "claim" is the spec verb (symmetric with `quote`), and the function returns the payout for a claim. (3) Widen the `_input: unknown` type to a structured `{ policy, incident: { damages, ... } }` shape — rejected on cycle-13 precedent: types should grow when fields are actually consumed; no field is read yet, so a typed shape would be speculative. (4) Extract `400` into a named module constant (e.g. `STANDARD_PAYOUT`) — rejected: `400` is the test-specific total of `500 − 100`, not a domain constant; naming it would be a lie since it has no domain identity. The parameter underscore prefix `_input` correctly signals "intentionally unused for now" per TS lint convention. Mass unchanged (2). All existing `quote`-related names and structure unchanged. `claim` name fits.
82	
83	## After cycle 19
84	- Change: extracted module-level constant `DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM = 100` (placed after the `PROCESSING_FEE` block, beginning a new claim-policy section), replacing the literal `100` in `claim`'s body. Function body now reads `return input.incident.damages[0].amount - DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM;`.
85	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `100` is the domain "per-item deductible" per spec R-claim ("standard reimbursement: damage − 100 G deductible"). Cycle 18 deferred this extraction on a "single data point" objection; cycle 19's test (damaged rune 200 → payout 100) is the SECOND independent confirmation that the deductible is `100` regardless of item type or damage amount, satisfying the two-data-point threshold. Naming the constant now mirrors spec vocabulary and prepares for cycle 20's forcing test (dragon attack: 500 + 300 → 600 = (500-100) + (300-100)), where the per-item *application* (per-damage-entry reduce) will be added but the *amount* (100) remains the same. Kept the single-element `damages[0]` access — the per-item reduce shape is still cycle-20 work; today's commitment is only the named amount. Considered also extracting a `payoutForDamage(damage) = damage.amount - DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM` helper to anticipate cycle 20's reduce — rejected as speculative; cycle-20 Green will introduce the reduce naturally and the helper can be extracted then if Rule 2 demands it. Considered renaming `claim` → `payout` — rejected on cycle-18 precedent: "claim" is the spec verb. Considered widening the type signature — already typed correctly in cycle-18's Green signature (`{ policy: unknown; incident: { cause?: string; damages: { itemType: string; amount: number }[] } }`), no change needed. Mass change: ~+1 (one new module-level constant binding) with a literal → named-binding swap (mass-neutral on the swap, +1 from the new module binding). Accepted on cycle-14/15/16/17 precedent (Rule 2 > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the value). The new constant placement (after `PROCESSING_FEE`, separated by a blank line) signals the start of a new claim-side policy-constants block (cycle 21+ will likely add `DRAGON_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE = 1.0`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_PAYOUT_RATE = 0.5` in the same block).
86	- Naming established: `DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM` (module-private constant, replaces literal `100`); `claim` and all prior `quote`-related names unchanged.
87	
88	## After cycle 20
89	- Change: renamed the reducer accumulator parameter in `claim`'s body from `sum` to `totalPayout`. Function body now reads `return input.incident.damages.reduce((totalPayout, damage) => totalPayout + (damage.amount - DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM), 0);`. No structural changes.
90	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `sum` names the mechanical operation; `totalPayout` names the domain concept (the running total of payouts per spec R-claim). Mass unchanged (pure rename). Considered four larger refactorings, rejected each: (1) Extract `payoutForDamage(damage) = damage.amount - DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM` helper — cycle-19 explicitly deferred this to cycle 20 "if Rule 2 demands it"; with only the standard deductible rule active, the inline expression is short and self-documenting via the named constant, and Rule 4 (fewest elements) discourages abstraction without clear purpose. The genuine forcing test is the next todo ("dragon-material sword → full reimbursement, then deductible"), which will branch the per-damage calculation and force a named helper to host the branching logic; doing it now would commit to a shape (`damage.amount` is the only signal) that the dragon clause needs to extend with policy-item lookups. (2) Extract `const damages = input.incident.damages` (cycle-9 precedent) — rejected: cycle-9 was justified by THREE repeated `input.items` accesses; `claim` currently has only ONE `input.incident.damages` access. Will be justified when cycle 21+ adds a second access path (joining `damages[i].itemType` with `policy.items[j].type`). (3) Widen `policy: unknown` to a structured type — rejected on cycle-13 precedent: types should grow when fields are actually consumed; no field of `policy` is read yet. (4) Rename `claim` → `computePayout` — rejected on cycle-18 precedent: "claim" is the spec verb. (5) Extract `0` (reduce initial value) into a named constant — rejected: `0` is the additive identity, not a domain constant.
91	- Naming established: `totalPayout` (reduce accumulator parameter, replaces mechanical `sum`); all other names unchanged.
92	
93	## After cycle 21
94	- Change: three coordinated improvements after the high-enchantment-claim forcing test (dragon-material sword ench exactly 8, damage 1000 → payout 400) went green. (1) Extracted module-level `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD = 8` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_PAYOUT_RATE = 0.5` (placed in the claim-policy block after `DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM`), replacing the magic literals `8` and `0.5` in the ternary. (2) Extracted local aliases `const damages = input.incident.damages` and `const policyItems = input.policy.items` at the top of `claim`'s body — cycle 20 explicitly deferred this with "will be justified when cycle 21+ adds a second access path (joining damages[i].itemType with policy.items[j].type)"; that path now exists (the `find` call), triggering the cycle-9 aliasing precedent. (3) Extracted local predicate `const hasHighEnchantment = (policyItem?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD` and renamed `item`/`i` → `policyItem`/`item` (callback param) to disambiguate the policy-side item from the incident-side damage entry; the ternary now reads `hasHighEnchantment ? damage.amount * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_PAYOUT_RATE : damage.amount`.
95	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the cycle-21 Green left `8` (high-enchantment claim threshold per spec R-claim) and `0.5` (50% payout rate per spec R-claim) as bare literals, asymmetric with the named `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5` / `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` extracted in cycle 14 on the quote side. Test name reads "high-ench clause wins: 50% then deductible"; spec calls it "enchantment level ≥ 8 → 50% payout"; both values earn names mirroring the spec vocabulary. The new constant name explicitly carries the `_CLAIM_` qualifier because the quote-side threshold is 5 (different domain value despite shared "high enchantment" phrasing); this prevents future confusion when both thresholds coexist. Local predicate `hasHighEnchantment` names the eligibility check as its own domain concept (cycle-22's TODO test "ench 9, dragon-material" reuses this exact predicate semantically; cycle 23's "ench 9 steel" will exercise the same branch). The `policyItem`/`item` rename disambiguates the two item collections (`policyItems` from the policy vs. the loop variable in `find`); the previous `item`/`i` shadow was a smell. Also Rule 3 (no duplication): aliasing `damages` and `policyItems` mirrors cycle-9's `items` aliasing in `quote`, and the two access paths (`input.incident.damages` for the reduce + `input.policy.items` for the find lookup) are exactly the two-access threshold cycle-20 named as the trigger. Considered five larger refactorings, rejected each: (1) Extract `payoutForDamage(damage, policyItem)` helper — cycle-20 deferred this and the genuine forcing context (multiple branches) is still mostly future work: the next two TODOs (ench-9 dragon vs. ench-9 steel) confirm the existing high-ench branch without adding new branches; the fourth TODO (rounding for ench-9 dragon damage 1001) might force a `Math.floor` wrapper but still in the same single-branch shape; only when an explicit dragon-clause branch separates from the high-ench branch (no test forces this yet) would a helper become structurally necessary. The current 4-line callback body is still readable inline. Per cycle-8/10/20 precedent: helpers extract when used 2+ times or when locality is hurt by branching complexity. (2) Generalise the ternary into an array of `(damage, policyItem) → reimbursableAmount` rule functions iterated to find the matching one — pure speculation; spec has only one rule fork active. (3) Note that the dragon test (cycle 20, "dragon-material ench 5 → full reimbursement") passes despite `cause: "dragon"` and `material: "dragon"` being unread — the full reimbursement is an emergent consequence of `enchantment 5 < HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD` flowing through the else branch. The dragon clause is observationally INDISTINGUISHABLE from "no special rule applies" at all data points seen so far; explicitly implementing it would be cargo-culted code with no test signal. The cycle-22 todo (ench 9 dragon vs. ench 9 steel BOTH paying 400) is the negative-confirming test that the high-enchantment clause WINS when both apply — and again no positive signal forces a separate dragon path. (4) Rename `claim` → `computePayout` — rejected on cycle-18/20 precedent ("claim" is the spec verb). (5) Widen the `0` reduce initial into a named constant `STARTING_PAYOUT` — rejected on cycle-20 precedent (`0` is the additive identity). Mass change: ~+5 local (3 new local bindings: damages, policyItems, hasHighEnchantment + 2 callback-param renames are mass-neutral) and +2 module (two named constants); literals→named-bindings swap is mass-neutral. Accepted on cycle-14/15/16/17 precedent (Rule 2 > APP when spec/test vocabulary names the value).
96	- Naming established: `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD` (module-private constant, replaces literal `8`; the `_CLAIM_` qualifier distinguishes from the quote-side `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_PAYOUT_RATE` (module-private constant, replaces literal `0.5`), `damages` (local alias for `input.incident.damages`), `policyItems` (local alias for `input.policy.items`), `hasHighEnchantment` (local predicate binding), `policyItem` (local from `find`, replaces ambiguous `item`); `claim`, `totalPayout`, `reimbursable`, `DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM`, and all `quote`-related names unchanged.
97

204:- Discrepancies: none. This is the genuine FORCING test for R13 (high-enchantment payout: 50% reimbursement for ench ≥ 8) AND R15 (precedence: R13 wins when both R13 and R14 apply). Two correctness checks for the cycle-26 Green: cycle 22 (steel sword ench 3 dmg 500 → 400 = standard) and cycle 25 (dragon sword ench 5 dmg 800 → 700 = standard) must stay green — both have ench < HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_PAYOUT_THRESHOLD (likely 8), so they take the standard branch. The full clause-precedence matrix is pinned by cycles 25-28: (no clause)→standard, (R14 only, ench<8)→standard-equivalent, (R13 only, ench≥8 + steel)→400, (R13+R14, ench≥8 + dragon)→400 via R15.

content.text:
All 30 tests pass. Let me now evaluate the current state for refactoring.

## Refactor Phase Analysis (Cycle 30)

The recent Green wrapped `totalPayout` in `Math.floor` — was a 1-line change. Let me inspect the `claim` function carefully:

```ts
export function claim(input: {...}): number {
  const damages = input.incident.damages;
  const policyItems = input.policy.items;
  const totalPayout = damages.reduce((totalPayout, damage) => {
    const policyItem = policyItems.find((item) => item.type === damage.itemType);
    const hasHighEnchantment = (policyItem?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD;
    const reimbursable = hasHighEnchantment ? damage.amount * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_PAYOUT_RATE : damage.amount;
    return totalPayout + (reimbursable - DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM);
  }, 0);
  return Math.floor(totalPayout);
}
```

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

- `claim` — spec verb, returns payout amount. Keep.
- `totalPayout` (outer binding and reducer accumulator) — clear domain noun, mirrors `premium` in `quote`. Shadows itself: outer `const totalPayout` is the reduce result, inner reducer param also `totalPayout`. **This is shadow-naming**. The outer name describes "the final accumulated payout"; the inner name describes "the running accumulator". They're the same concept at different stages. Acceptable but creates a subtle smell.
- `damages`, `policyItems`, `policyItem`, `hasHighEnchantment`, `reimbursable` — all clear, spec-aligned.
- The Math.floor introduction in cycle 30: the `totalPayout` outer binding stores the pre-rounded sum. **Could rename to `rawTotalPayout`** to make the rounding step's purpose more visible, mirroring the cycle-6 `premium` pattern in `quote` where the pre-rounded value gets a distinct name. But actually in `quote`, `premium` IS the pre-rounded value and Math.ceil wraps it directly. So the same pattern is used here.

The shadow naming (outer `totalPayout` = inner accumulator name) is the real smell. Let me consider renaming the outer to make the rounding step read more clearly. Per cycle-6 precedent: `premium` is the named pre-rounded value in `quote`, and Math.ceil(premium) reads as "round up the premium". Here `Math.floor(totalPayout)` reads as "round down the total payout", which is fine — but the inner accumulator also called `totalPayout` is the *running* total, not the final. 

### Step 2: Initial APP Mass (claim function only, since that's what changed)

```
const damages = input.incident.damages;           // binding 1, invocation 0, member access ~2 invocations: 1+2 = 3
const policyItems = input.policy.items;           // 1+2 = 3
const totalPayout = damages.reduce((totalPayout, damage) => {   // binding 1, invocation 1 (reduce) = 3
  const policyItem = policyItems.find((item) => item.type === damage.itemType); // binding 1, invocation 1, conditional via === (counts as constant cmp), 2 bindings (item param, accesses), constant 0
  const hasHighEnchantment = (policyItem?.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD; // binding 1, conditional 4 (?? and >=), constant 1
  const reimbursable = hasHighEnchantment ? damage.amount * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_PAYOUT_RATE : damage.amount; // binding 1, conditional 4
  return totalPayout + (reimbursable - DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM);
}, 0);
return Math.floor(totalPayout);                  // invocation 2
```

Rough mass: ~30. Math.floor adds 2 (invocation). Cycle 30 added only 1 element (the Math.floor call) — minimal change.

### Step 3: Evaluate Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests pass):** ✅ All 30 pass.

**Rule 2 (Reveals intent):** The shadow-naming `totalPayout` outer + `totalPayout` reducer-param is mildly confusing. However, the cycle-30 rationale is: the outer is the pre-rounded total, the inner is the running sum. They're the same conceptual thing at different "ticks". An alternative naming:
- Rename outer to `rawTotalPayout` so `Math.floor(rawTotalPayout)` makes rounding intent explicit.

But: cycle-6 precedent in `quote` uses `premium` for the pre-rounded value (NOT `rawPremium`), with `Math.ceil(premium)` reading as "round up the premium". So consistency with `quote` suggests keeping `totalPayout` as the pre-rounded name. The shadow with the inner reducer param is a separate concern; renaming the **inner** reducer param to `runningTotal` (or similar) would resolve the shadow while preserving the cycle-6 pattern.

Actually wait — the cycle-20 change was: "renamed the reducer accumulator parameter from `sum` to `totalPayout`". And cycle-30 introduced the outer binding (Green added `const totalPayout = ...` and `Math.floor(totalPayout)`). So we now have shadow naming as a direct consequence of cycle-30 + cycle-20. This is a real smell worth fixing.

Options:
- (a) Rename inner reducer param back to `sum` (reverses cycle 20 — undoes a Rule-2 improvement)
- (b) Rename inner to `runningTotal` (clarifies it's mid-iteration; outer `totalPayout` is the final value)
- (c) Rename outer to `rawTotalPayout` (mirrors none of our precedents; cycle 6's `premium` is unwrapped)
- (d) Inline outer: `return Math.floor(damages.reduce(...))` — eliminates shadow AND a binding (Rule 4 win)

Option (d) is interesting. In `quote`, `premium` survives as a named binding because (per cycle 6) "rounding step deserves to be a visible named operation". But in `quote`, the expression building `premium` is a 6-summand line — naming it makes the rounding step legible. In `claim`, the `damages.reduce(...)` is already a self-contained named operation. Inlining doesn't lose intent.

Wait — let me reconsider. The outer binding's value here is the *aggregated total payout across all damages*. It deserves a name. Let me think about this differently. The shadow is the smell — fix the shadow, keep both names.

**Rule 3 (No duplication):** The two surcharge accumulators in `quote` could be merged into one loop branch — but cycle-15 explicitly rejected that, keeping the per-policy clauses visually separate. No new duplication introduced this cycle.

**Rule 4 (Fewest elements):** Could inline `rawTotalPayout`/outer `totalPayout` — but that's option (d) above which conflicts with Rule 2 (giving the pre-rounded sum a domain name). Per cycle-6 precedent, the named pre-rounded value wins.

### Step 4: Decision

Pick option (b): rename the **inner** reducer accumulator from `totalPayout` to `runningTotal`. This:
- Resolves the shadow (clear distinction between outer "final total payout" and inner "running total during reduce")
- Preserves cycle-6 precedent (named pre-rounded value in the outer binding)
- Improves Rule 2 (the inner is *mid-iteration*; the outer is *final*)
- Mass-neutral

Wait — but `runningTotal` is mechanical (like `sum` was). Cycle 20 explicitly renamed `sum` → `totalPayout` to favor domain-noun over mechanical-noun. Reverting to a mechanical name violates cycle-20's rationale.

Alternative: rename the **outer** to `grossPayout` (suggesting "before rounding") and keep inner as `totalPayout`. But `grossPayout` introduces vocabulary not in the spec.

Best option: **rename the outer binding to be more specific about its role**. The outer represents the *pre-rounded sum*. Spec doesn't have a vocabulary word here, but the cycle-6 precedent (in `quote`) uses `premium` (the unrounded value, then `Math.ceil(premium)`). The symmetric word here would be `totalPayout`, with rounding outside.

Hmm. Let me check `quote` again: it has `const premium = ...` then `return Math.ceil(premium);`. The reducer accumulator inside the `for` loop in `quote` is... actually `quote` uses `for` loops with `let basePrice = 0; basePrice += ...`, no `.reduce()` accumulator name. So there's no shadow precedent.

I'll go with option (b) but use a domain-sympathetic name: rename the inner accumulator from `totalPayout` to `payoutSoFar`. This names the mid-iteration value as "payout accumulated so far in the loop" — domain noun + temporal qualifier, distinct from the final `totalPayout`.

Actually, the cleanest fix: rename the **inner** to `accumulated` or just resolve by inlining differently. Let me try yet another angle: in cycle-9 we extracted `const items = input.items` to remove repeated accesses; the outer-totalPayout-then-Math.floor pattern is structurally analogous to "store result of reduce in a binding so the outer expression is one named step". 

Let me commit to: **rename inner accumulator to `payoutSoFar`** — this is a domain-noun with a clear temporal qualifier, mirrors cycle-20's choice of domain-over-mechanical, and resolves the shadow.

### Step 5: Implement

input.old_string: - Naming established: `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD` (module-private constant, replaces literal `8`; the `_CLAIM_` qualifier distinguishes from the quote-side `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_PAYOUT_RATE` (module-private constant, replaces literal `0.5`), `damages` (local alias for `input.incident.damages`), `policyItems` (local alias for `input.policy.items`), `hasHighEnchantment` (local predicate binding), `policyItem` (local from `find`, replaces ambiguous `item`); `claim`, `totalPayout`, `reimbursable`, `DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM`, and all `quote`-related names unchanged.
input.new_string:
- Naming established: `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD` (module-private constant, replaces literal `8`; the `_CLAIM_` qualifier distinguishes from the quote-side `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`), `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_PAYOUT_RATE` (module-private constant, replaces literal `0.5`), `damages` (local alias for `input.incident.damages`), `policyItems` (local alias for `input.policy.items`), `hasHighEnchantment` (local predicate binding), `policyItem` (local from `find`, replaces ambiguous `item`); `claim`, `totalPayout`, `reimbursable`, `DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM`, and all `quote`-related names unchanged.

## After cycle 30
- Change: renamed the reduce accumulator parameter in `claim` from `totalPayout` to `payoutSoFar`. Inner reducer callback now reads `(payoutSoFar, damage) => ... return payoutSoFar + (reimbursable - DEDUCTIBLE_PER_ITEM)`. The outer binding `const totalPayout = damages.reduce(...)` and the rounding step `return Math.floor(totalPayout)` (cycle-30 Green) are unchanged.
- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the cycle-30 Green introduced an outer `const totalPayout = damages.reduce(...)` binding so the rounding step could read `Math.floor(totalPayout)` (mirroring the cycle-6 `Math.ceil(premium)` pattern in `quote`). This created a name shadow: the outer `totalPayout` (final pre-rounded sum) and the inner reduce-accumulator `totalPayout` (introduced in cycle 20 as a domain-noun improvement over the mechanical `sum`) shared a name despite representing distinct concepts — the mid-iteration running value vs. the completed total. The new name `payoutSoFar` preserves cycle-20's domain-noun-over-mechanical-noun principle (rejecting reverting to `sum`) while adding a temporal qualifier ("so far") that distinguishes the mid-loop accumulator from the final total. The outer name `totalPayout` is preserved because it directly mirrors `premium` in `quote` (cycle-6: a named pre-rounded value before `Math.ceil`/`Math.floor`). Mass change: 0 (pure rename — two occurrences). Considered four alternatives, rejected each: (1) Inline the outer binding (`return Math.floor(damages.reduce(...))`) to eliminate the shadow via Rule 4 (fewest elements) — rejected on cycle-6 precedent: the pre-rounded sum deserves a named binding so the rounding step is a visible, named operation, even when the inner expression is already a `.reduce()` call (the reduce names the *operation*, the outer binding names the *result* it produces). (2) Rename outer to `rawTotalPayout` to make rounding more explicit — rejected: introduces vocabulary asymmetric with `quote`'s `premium`/`Math.ceil(premium)` pattern; `totalPayout`/`Math.floor(totalPayout)` should read the same way. (3) Revert inner to `sum` — rejected: undoes cycle 20's deliberate domain-noun improvement. (4) Extract a `payoutForDamage(damage, policyItem)` helper to host the reducer body — cycle-20 and cycle-21 both deferred this; the 3-line callback body is still readable inline and no second caller exists. The forcing test for that helper has still not arrived; the upcoming CLI cycles (31-38) are integration concerns that don't reshape `claim`'s internal structure.
- Naming established: `payoutSoFar` (reduce accumulator parameter, replaces shadowed `totalPayout`); `totalPayout` (outer pre-rounded binding, unchanged), `claim`, and all prior constants/locals unchanged.

Refactor Phase Complete.

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Naming (Rule 2): Renamed the reduce-accumulator parameter in `claim` from `totalPayout` to `payoutSoFar` to resolve the name shadow created when cycle-30 Green introduced an outer `const totalPayout = damages.reduce(...)` binding wrapping `Math.floor(...)`.
- The outer `totalPayout` (final pre-rounded total) is preserved — it mirrors `premium` in `quote` per the cycle-6 precedent (a named pre-rounded value before rounding).
- The inner `payoutSoFar` preserves cycle-20's domain-noun-over-mechanical-noun principle (rejected reverting to `sum`) while adding a temporal qualifier that distinguishes mid-iteration from final value.

**Mass Change:** 0 (pure rename, two occurrences).
